text: Unsaturated fats can easily become rancid due to a process called oxidation. This makes them turn color and develop unpleasant tastes or smells. That's why these foods aren't usually preserved by drying; instead, they are often smoked, salted, or fermented. Even fruits with less fat get sprayed with antioxidants before being dried. Metals can speed up oxidation. Some oils like olive oil have natural antioxidants that help protect them from becoming rancid. Fatty foods are sensitive to light-induced oxidation, which creates harmful compounds called hydroperoxides. Exposure to sunlight can directly cause this oxidation and break down other harmful molecules. These molecules can start a chain reaction, but antioxidants stop them by preventing the oxidation process.
label: human

text: The 2014 Super Bowl, known as Super Bowl XLVIII, was the NFL's big annual football championship game. It happened on February 2nd at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. The Seattle Seahawks won against the Denver Broncos with a score of 43-8, setting records for the largest victory margin and most points scored in one quarter (88 yards in the second). Russell Wilson, the Seahawks' quarterback, threw two touchdown passes, ran for another, but also got intercepted once. The Broncos missed chances to score early on, letting Seattle take a big lead they kept throughout the game. This was the first time the Seattle Seahawks won a Super Bowl, and it came after an amazing playoff run where they beat three teams by a total of 149-30.

text: Lal Behari Dey was born on December 18, 1824, in Sonapalasi near Bardhaman, to a Bengali family belonging to the Suvarna Banik caste. His father, Radhakanta Dey Mondal, worked as a small bill broker in Kolkata. After learning basics in his village school, he moved to Calcutta with his father and joined Reverend Alexander Duff's General Assembly Institution for further studies from 1834 to 1844. This institution is now known as the Scottish Church Collegiate School. He was one of the first five students admitted by Duff. Under Duff's guidance, Dey officially became a Christian on July 2, 1843. A year before his baptism, in 1842, he wrote and published a booklet titled "The Falsity of the Hindu Religion," which won an award for the best essay from a local Christian society.

text: Alboin was a king of the Lombards, ruling from around 546 to 572 AD. He's an important figure in Lombard history, shaping their kingdom significantly. Born about 526 AD, Alboin led his people out of Pannonia, where they were settled by the Huns. They moved their capital to Ticinum (now Pavia). Alboin expanded Lombard territories through battles against nearby tribes and the Eastern Roman Empire, conquering much of Italy. His reign brought changes like adopting Arian Christianity and introducing Germanic customs in Italy. Historians see him as a unifier for his people or a ruthless conqueror who caused destruction in Italy.

text: Direct trading, known as barter, happens when people swap goods or services without using money. This method has been used for centuries across different cultures, especially in places where regular currency isn't available or trustworthy. For instance, a farmer might give extra wheat to a blacksmith in exchange for a new tool they need. Barter helps get necessary items like food, shelter, and clothes, but it can be tricky because finding someone with what you want who also wants what you have can be tough. Also, since there's no official authority overseeing these trades, trust between the parties involved is crucial for fair deals and keeping promises.
label: llm

text: A computer program is a collection of instructions a computer follows to complete a specific job or reach a certain goal. Written in a language computers understand, like Python or Java, these instructions are a series of steps for the computer's brain (CPU) to carry out. Programs can be as basic as a calculator app or as intricate as an entire operating system. They come in various sizes, from tiny scripts to big software applications. Stored in memory, programs run on the CPU and interact with users via devices such as keyboards, mice, and screens. They can also talk to other computers over a network. Created using different programming languages, these instructions are designed, coded, tested, and debugged during development to make sure they work correctly and efficiently.

text: The Acorn Electron is a British-made home computer launched in 1983 by Acorn Computers Ltd. It was designed to be an affordable and easy-to-use alternative to other computers available at the time. Similar to the BBC Micro, it used the same hardware but made adjustments for broader use. The Electron boasted a Zilog Z80A processor running at 2 MHz, 16 KB of memory, and an in-built BASIC programming language. It came with various input/output ports, including one for loading programs and data from audio cassettes. Marketed as an educational tool, it also attracted hobbyists due to its affordability and expandable features. Despite fierce competition during its release, the Electron maintained a dedicated fanbase and continued production until 1986.

text: Anne Brontë was born in 1820 as the third child of Patrick and Maria Brontë, who encouraged literature and education in their family. Her sisters Charlotte and Emily also became well-known writers. Anne started writing poetry but later moved on to novels. Her first novel, "Agnes Grey," came out in 1847 under a pseudonym and was based on her experiences as a governess. Her second book, "The Tenant of Wildfell Hall," published anonymously in 1848, was more successful than the first. Anne's writing style was simple and honest, much like her own straightforward nature. Despite battling poor health throughout her life, she continued to write until her death from tuberculosis at age 29 in 1849. Her novels are appreciated for their portrayal of the social issues during that time, particularly focusing on women's lives and working-class experiences.

text: Agesilaus II was a ruler of Sparta from 401 to 360 BCE, taking over after his brother Agis II. He was famous for his battles in Asia Minor against the Persian Empire. In 396 BCE, he won victories against two powerful leaders, Tissaphernes and Phamabazos, capturing several cities and controlling the region. However, he couldn't completely defeat the Persians. Agesilaus also made changes in Sparta, like altering land ownership rules and creating new military groups. His rule was filled with disputes from some Spartans. He passed away in 360 BCE, possibly due to sickness, but rumors say he might have been poisoned. Agesilaus' legacy is a mix of his military triumphs and the difficulties he faced as king.

Together they had four children: Lady Janet Helen (who married scientist Harold Shipton in 1947), Lady Felicity Ann (who wed business executive John Keith Harwood in 1955), Martin Richard, Viscount Prestwood, later the 2nd Earl Attlee (married Anne Henderson in 1955), and Lady Alison Elizabeth (who married Richard Davis in 1952).

text: Abiotic stress refers to problems faced by living organisms due to harsh conditions in their environment, caused by things like extreme temperatures, dryness, salty soil, or intense light. For example, too much heat or cold can shock plants, while a lack of water can slow down their growth. High salt levels in the soil can disrupt how plants take up nutrients and balance ions. Even too much or too little light can affect plant growth, reducing photosynthesis and productivity. These stresses can seriously impact crop yields, plant survival, and the overall health of ecosystems. That's why it's crucial to understand how these stressors work so we can create strategies to help plants thrive in tough environments.
label: llm

text: Astatine, a rare and radioactive element, is mostly made by hitting bismuth-209 with powerful alpha particles in cyclotrons. This process creates small amounts of astatine-209, 210, and 211. Modern methods can produce up to 86 nanograms or about 2.47 trillion atoms at a time. However, making more astatine is tough due to the scarcity of big cyclotrons and the risk of melting the target material. Another issue is that astatine can cause problems when it decays in solvents. Using cryogenic technology, we might be able to create micrograms of astatine by bombarding thorium or uranium with protons, which then turn into radon-211, which later becomes astatine-211. Unfortunately, this method may also lead to unwanted astatine-210.
label: human

text: African-Americans are people living in the U.S., mostly with roots in Africa. They make up about 13% of America's population. Their story goes back to the slave trade between Africa and the Americas, which forced millions of Africans into slavery from the 16th to the 19th century. After the American Civil War ended slavery, African-Americans still faced tough challenges like segregation, losing the right to vote, and violence. Despite these hardships, they've made important contributions in areas such as music, art, literature, science, and politics. Today, they continue shaping America's culture, society, and identity. Their experiences are rich and complex, showing the different histories, cultures, and traditions from various African regions.

text: The Buffalo Bills are a well-known American football team based in Orchard Park, New York. Since 1959, they've been part of the National Football League (NFL), playing in the AFC East division. Their home games take place at New Era Field since 1973. Before the NFL and American Football League merged in 1970, the Bills won two championships. However, they haven't made it to a Super Bowl final game since then. Over the years, some fantastic players have donned the Bills' jersey, like Jim Kelly, Thurman Thomas, and Bruce Smith – all in the Pro Football Hall of Fame. Despite not winning a Super Bowl yet, the Bills are still a much-loved team with a rich history.

text: Jean-Robert Argand coined the term 'module' in French in 1806, which means a unit of measurement, particularly for complex numbers' size. This term was later adopted into English as 'modulus' in 1866. The term 'absolute value' has been used since 1806 in French and 1857 in English to describe this concept. Karl Weierstrass introduced the notation of absolute value, using a vertical bar on each side, in 1841. Absolute value is also known as numerical value or magnitude. In computer programming languages and software packages, the absolute value of a number is usually denoted by abs(x) or a similar expression.

text: Fort Douglas is a U.S. Army base situated in Salt Lake City, Utah. Built in 1862 during the Utah War, it was initially created to safeguard settlers from possible Mormon rebellion and assert federal control over the region. The fort got its name from Colonel Solomon Meredith's Indiana infantry regiment, famously known as the "Iron Brigade," which had fought at Gettysburg. In its early days, Fort Douglas functioned as a supply center and base for local military activities. By 1869, it transformed into a permanent post, playing a more crucial role in defending the region. Over time, the fort has gone through several changes, including shifts in its purpose and duties, but it continues to be an active Army installation today, hosting diverse units and offering support for western U.S. military operations.
label: llm

text: In the lives of many living things with multiple cells, called multicellular organisms, they create spores during their lifespan through a process known as sporogenesis. Most animals and certain one-celled creatures skip this step and go straight to meiosis followed by fertilization. However, plants and many seaweeds do something different – they undergo a type of meiosis that produces single-celled spores instead of gametes. These spores grow into individual organisms called gametophytes, without needing a fertilization event. Later on, these single-celled organisms reproduce by dividing their cells through mitosis and creating gametes. This means meiosis and the creation of gametes happen in different stages or phases of the life cycle, known as alternation of generations. Even though spore formation in plants and seaweeds might seem like a form of reproduction without fertilization (agamogenesis), it's actually still part of sexual reproduction because both events – spore formation and fertilization – are essential for the plant life cycle to be complete.
label: human

text: The Branchiopoda group includes creatures with gills on various appendages, like mouthparts. This is why they're named this way – 'branchio' means gills, similar to 'trachea', our windpipe, and 'pod' means foot. Most of them have compound eyes and a protective covering called carapace. This can be a two-valve shell surrounding the body (most Cladocera), broad and shallow (Notostraca), or completely missing (Anostraca). In some cases where the carapace hinders swimming with the trunk limbs (Cladocera, clam shrimp, and extinct Lipostraca), they use antennae for movement instead. Male fairy shrimp have enlarged antennae to hold females during mating. Notostraca bottom-feeders have reduced antennae to tiny remnants. The trunk limbs move in a synchronized rhythm, creating water flow along the animal's middle, providing oxygen, food, and for Anostraca and Notostraca, movement.
label: human

text: Avicenna's writings about astronomy influenced later thinkers, but his work wasn't as advanced as Alhazen or Al-Biruni's. One unique aspect is that he saw mathematical astronomy and astrology as separate fields. He disagreed with Aristotle's idea that stars get their light from the Sun, believing instead that they shine on their own. He also thought planets were self-luminous. Avicenna claimed to have seen Venus as a dot on the Sun, which could be true since there was a transit on May 24, 1032. However, he didn't mention when he made his observation, and scholars question if it was possible for him to see it from his location at that time. He might have mistaken a sunspot for Venus. His observation helped prove that Venus, at times, is below the Sun in the common geocentric model, meaning the sphere of Venus comes before the Sun's sphere when moving out from Earth.
label: human

text: Many problems can be solved by changing their form to match ANOVA's assumptions. One important assumption, called unit-treatment additivity, can sometimes be achieved through transformations. This is necessary when the data doesn't follow a straightforward pattern of change. If the data follows a specific type of distribution, statisticians might choose to adjust the data to make the variation more consistent. For instance, they may opt for transformations like taking the logarithm if they believe the data follows a multiplicative model. According to Cauchy's functional equation theorem, only the logarithm can convert real-world multiplication into addition when it comes to continuous transformations.
label: human

text: In June 2001, the UK held a general election, where Tony Blair's Labour Party won again, having been in power since 1997. Many people voted - over half of those eligible! The Conservative Party, led by William Hague, and the Liberal Democrats, with Charles Kennedy at the helm, were the main opponents. The election was about important topics like economy, public services, and foreign affairs. Labour promised to keep spending on things like healthcare and education. They also stressed their role in keeping Britain safe after the 9/11 attacks in America. In the end, Labour stayed in power for a third term, but with fewer seats in Parliament than before.

text: Angola, found in southern Africa, shares borders with Namibia to the south, Zambia and Democratic Republic of Congo to the north, and the Atlantic Ocean to the west. This country boasts a varied landscape, featuring mountains, plateaus, and savannas across its expanse. The capital city, Luanda, is located on the coast and is Angola's largest urban hub. After a long fight for independence from Portugal in 1975, Angola faced challenges such as civil war and instability but has recently made impressive economic strides. Abundant natural resources like oil, diamonds, and iron ore have fueled this growth. The majority of Angolans are Bantu-speaking people, with smaller communities of Portuguese, Chinese, and others due to historical migrations. Portuguese is the official language, but many also speak local tongues such as Kimbundu and Kikongo.
label: llm

text: Amdahl's Law is a rule that helps predict how fast a computer program can run when it's split into parts and worked on by multiple processors at once. This idea was first thought up by Gene Amdahl in 1967. The law says that a program's speed will be limited by the part of it that can't be divided for parallel processing, which we call the 'serial fraction'. Even if most of a program can be broken down into tasks that multiple processors can work on at the same time, the total speed increase is still limited by the part that needs to run one after the other. This law is usually written as S = 1 / (1 - p) + p/n, where S is the maximum possible speedup, p is the serial fraction of the program, and n is the number of processors available. This equation shows that as more processors are added, the potential for speeding up the program decreases due to the limitations set by its sequential part.
label: llm

text: Gaelic speakers, however, tended to be poor; they were largely illiterate and had little access to education. This situation persisted into the early days of the twentieth century. In 1921 Gaelic was approved as an optional subject in the curriculum of Nova Scotia, but few teachers could be found and children were discouraged from using the language in schools. By 1931 the number of Gaelic speakers in Nova Scotia had fallen to approximately 25,000, mostly in discrete pockets. In Cape Breton it was still a majority language, but the proportion was falling. Children were no longer being raised with Gaelic.
label: human

text: A bird is a kind of animal that's warm-blooded and has feathers, beaks, hollow bones, and three-toed feet with sharp claws. Feathers help keep them warm, support flight, and serve for eating, grooming, and other tasks. Hollow bones make flying easier, while their feet are perfect for walking on different surfaces. Most birds lay eggs outside their bodies (oviparous), but some like the kiwi develop young inside eggs that hatch within them (ovoviviparous). Birds live in various places, from deserts to rainforests, and come in all sizes. The smallest bird is the bee hummingbird, about 5 centimeters long and 1.6 grams heavy, while the largest is the ostrich, reaching up to 2.8 meters tall and weighing up to 156 kilograms. Birds are known for their ability to travel long distances (migrate), communicate through songs and sounds, and show complex social behaviors.

text: Cornwall, situated at England's southwest tip, is surrounded by the Atlantic Ocean and shares borders with Devon to the north and east, and the Isles of Scilly to the southwest. Once an independent kingdom, it became part of England in 936 AD, preserving a unique Celtic culture. Tourism, agriculture, and mining are the main economic drivers, thanks to rich deposits of tin and copper. The region's dramatic coastline and beautiful landscapes attract millions of tourists annually, with popular spots like the Eden Project and St Michael's Mount. Although not widely spoken today, Cornish is recognized as a minority language by the UK government under the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages.

text: Elements that are chemically pure can connect together in various ways, resulting in the element existing in several different chemical structures called allotropes. These allotropes have unique characteristics. For instance, carbon can be a diamond, where each carbon atom has a tetrahedral structure around it. It can also be graphite, which consists of layers of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal pattern stacked on top of each other. Graphene is a single layer of graphite and is incredibly strong. Graphite can also form into graphene oxide. Carbon can take on nearly spherical shapes as fullerenes. Lastly, carbon nanotubes are tubes with a hexagonal structure, and they can vary in their electrical properties. This ability for an element to exist in multiple structural forms is known as allotropy.
label: human

text: In the Hebrew Bible and Christian Old Testament, there's a story about a man named Jonah. He was chosen by God to tell the people of Nineveh they were in trouble. But Jonah didn't want to do it, so he tried to run away on a ship to Tarshish instead. However, things didn't go as planned – a big fish swallowed him! After being spit back out on shore, Jonah finally went to Nineveh. He warned the people that God was angry with them because of their bad behavior and that disaster was coming if they didn't change. The people were sorry for what they had done and begged God for forgiveness. This led to a big change of heart among them, and God decided not to destroy Nineveh after all. Jonah wasn't happy about this because he thought the people shouldn't have been spared. The story shows Jonah's complicated feelings towards what God wanted him to do.

text: In ancient times, a man named Ambrose stood out for his efforts to help his fellow Romans, not just as a religious leader but also as a compassionate citizen. He cared deeply about the well-being of all people, especially the poor, viewing them as part of one community deserving of equal support. To Ambrose, giving to the needy wasn't merely an act of kindness, but a way to restore what rightfully belonged to everyone – resources given by God equally. He believed that the rich had taken more than their fair share, and justice meant ensuring the poor, whom he considered his "family," were provided for.

text: Copenhagen, Denmark's capital and largest city, sits on Zealand island's eastern coast. Inside its borders live about 623,000 people, while over 1.3 million call the metro area home. Originally a Viking fishing village from the 10th century, Copenhagen has transformed into a significant urban center and economic powerhouse. The city boasts well-preserved medieval architecture, such as Tivoli Gardens amusement park and Rosenborg Castle, housing Denmark's Crown Jewels. Copenhagen is also home to cultural gems like the National Museum of Denmark and Royal Danish Theatre. Its harbor bustles with commerce, welcoming cargo ships and cruise liners daily. Various organizations have recognized Copenhagen as one of the world's most livable cities due to its high quality of life, low crime rate, and efficient public transportation system.

text: The Book of the Eparch, which dates to the 10th century, gives a detailed picture of the city's commercial life and its organization at that time. The corporations in which the tradesmen of Constantinople were organised were supervised by the Eparch, who regulated such matters as production, prices, import, and export. Each guild had its own monopoly, and tradesmen might not belong to more than one. It is an impressive testament to the strength of tradition how little these arrangements had changed since the office, then known by the Latin version of its title, had been set up in 330 to mirror the urban prefecture of Rome.

text: Ammianus Marcellinus was a Roman historian and soldier who lived around 330 AD in Antioch. He fought as a military leader under Emperor Julian during the Gothic War in the late 4th century. After his military service, he wrote a history of Rome from Nerva's reign (96-98 AD) to Theodosius I (378-95 AD). His work, called "Res Gestae," is one of the key sources for understanding late antiquity. It's written in Latin and has 31 books, covering politics, wars, and social matters. Ammianus's writing is known for exciting battle descriptions and critical evaluations of Roman leaders and institutions. Despite being a soldier, his work stands out for its fairness and lack of bias, making it useful for historians studying the complexities of late ancient Rome.

text: Introduced by Korg in 1999, the MS2000 is a powerful music workstation synthesizer designed to cater to the needs of musicians, producers, and composers. This amazing device boasts a high-quality sound production system using a 32-bit floating-point signal processing technology. It comes packed with an extensive library of sounds, including analog-style emulations, digital waveforms, and sampled sounds. The MS2000 also offers various effects processors like reverb, delay, and distortion to help you customize or completely alter the sound. Moreover, it features a sequencer for recording and editing music on up to 16 tracks. Plus, it has a MIDI interface that allows control over external devices and seamless integration with other equipment. In essence, the Korg MS2000 is a versatile tool in electronic music production, offering numerous creative possibilities.

text: The Clásicos de la Provincia is a fun football tournament held in Argentina every year (except some times). It invites teams from different provinces across the country to play. This event started back in 1963 and has been organized by the Argentine Football Association since then. Usually, it happens during summer months. Teams compete in rounds first, then move on to a knockout stage. The special thing about this tournament is that most teams here don't play in Argentina's top league, Primera División. This means we get to see great regional football talent! Sometimes, the event gets postponed due to scheduling problems or other issues. Over time, Clásicos de la Provincia has given smaller province teams a chance to compete against each other and gain attention in Argentine football.

text: Bud Selig is an American businessman who was the commissioner of Major League Baseball (MLB) from 1998 to 2015. Born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin on July 30, 1934, he studied business administration at the University of Wisconsin-Madison. In 1969, Selig bought a baseball team called the Seattle Pilots and moved it to Milwaukee, where it became known as the Brewers. He led the Brewers as president until 1998 when he took over as acting commissioner of MLB. In 2003, Selig was officially elected as the ninth commissioner of MLB. During his time in office, he made important changes to the game like adding interleague play and reorganizing the league's divisions. He also helped create a steroid testing program and played a big part in moving teams, such as the Montreal Expos to Washington D.C. After 17 years, Selig retired from his position in 2015.

text: Lagoon Clipperton is a circular island surrounded by a still, freshwater lagoon in the eastern Pacific Ocean. It's unique as it's the only coral island in this region. The lagoon lacks fish and has shallow areas over eroded coral heads, but also deep basins with depths reaching 100 meters, like a famous spot called 'the bottomless hole'. This area has acidic water at its base. The water is nearly fresh on the surface but becomes highly nutrient-rich deeper down. About 45% of the lagoon's surface is covered in seaweed. The island's edge typically measures around a certain length, widening to its maximum in the west and narrowing towards the northeast, where waves from the sea sometimes splash into the lagoon. There are ten small islands within the lagoon, six of which have vegetation, including the Egg Islands.

text: A ballad is a type of story told through poetry or song. It's usually about legends, history, or myths and has a strong beat that makes it musical. The structure is usually four-line verses, like ABAB or ABCB, and the story is straightforward with little extra details. Ballads can be sung or spoken out loud, and their tunes are often easy to remember. They've been shared for centuries as a way to pass down stories and traditions from one generation to another. The ballad style has also affected other types of writing, like novels and plays, which often include storytelling and folklore themes.

text: The translation of the Kitáb-i-ʻAhd contains an error because the words 'Akbar' and 'Aʻzam' don't mean 'Greater' and 'Most Great' as commonly understood. These words come from different roots in Arabic - 'Akbar' from k-b-r and 'Aʻzam' from ʻ-z-m. Moreover, the Arabic language has a system called the elative, which doesn't clearly separate comparatives and superlatives. In Will and Testament, Muhammad ʻAlí, ʻAbdu'l-Bahá's half-brother, was mentioned below him in rank. Jealous of his brother, Muhammad ʻAlí sought power as an alternative leader with the help of his brothers Badiʻu'llah and Ḍíyáʼu'llah. He secretly wrote to Baháʼís in Iran, sowing doubts about ʻAbdu'l-Bahá. Although most Baháʼís followed ʻAbdu'l-Bahá, some, including leaders like Mirza Javad and Ibrahim George Kheiralla, a pioneer Baháʼí missionary in America, chose to follow Muhammad ʻAlí instead.

text: Ada Lovelace, born Augusta Ada Byron in 1815, is known as the world's first computer programmer. She came from an aristocratic family but was taught math and science, which was rare for women back then. Ada became friends with mathematician Charles Babbage, who built the Analytical Engine – an early type of computer. Ada translated an article about this machine by Luigi Menabrea and added her thoughts. Her notes showed that the machine could do more than just calculations; it could perform any task following a series of steps. This is now seen as describing a computer program. Ada's work on the Analytical Engine was published in 1843, making her the first to see computers could do more than simple math.
label: llm

text: Caspase proteins play a crucial role in the process called apoptosis, which is like a natural self-destruct mechanism for cells. When these proteins are removed (knock-outs), it can cause problems. For instance, removing caspase 9 causes severe brain malformations, while taking out caspase 8 leads to heart failure and early death of the embryo. However, using a special technology called cre-lox, scientists created a caspase 8 knock-out that has more T cells in the body, impaired T cell response, and issues with closing the neural tube. These mice are resistant to certain types of apoptosis but not others, like those triggered by UV light or chemotherapy drugs. A caspase 3 knock-out shows unusual cell masses in the brain and abnormal features during cell death, such as membrane bubbling or nuclear fragmentation. Interestingly, these KO mice have limited issues: removing caspases 3, 9, and a protein called APAF-1 affects neural tissue development, while taking out FADD and caspase 8 causes heart problems. But other organs develop normally in both types of KO, and some cell types are still sensitive to apoptotic signals, suggesting there might be unknown pathways that trigger cell death.
label: human

text: Axiology is a part of philosophy that looks at what we consider valuable and why. It includes studying things like good, bad, right, wrong, beautiful, and ugly, and how these ideas are understood across different cultures and societies. Axiologists also think about how values connect with other areas in philosophy, such as ethics (doing the right thing), aesthetics (appreciating beauty), and knowing what's true (epistemology). Axiology is split into two main parts: descriptive axiology, which tries to understand and organize existing values, and normative axiology, which wants to set rules for judging and justifying values. By examining various value systems, axiologists hope to gain a better understanding of the many ways people value things, how these values affect our actions, and their effect on society overall.
label: llm

text: BBC News is a 24/7 TV news channel run by the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC). Launched in 1997, it replaced the BBC's previous news shows on their main channels. The channel offers non-stop updates on both local and global news, emphasizing detailed explanations and live reports from various parts of the world. Shows include news flashes, discussions about current issues, and documentaries, along with coverage of significant events like elections, natural disasters, and wars. BBC News can be watched in the UK and worldwide through satellite TV, cable, and online streaming services. Viewers can also catch up on past programs using the BBC iPlayer service. As a public broadcaster, BBC News strives to deliver truthful and fair news, following the corporation's journalistic guidelines and standards.
label: llm

text: Assembly language is a type of programming language that talks directly to a computer's processor using symbols instead of numbers. It's translated into binary code by an assembler, which makes it ready for the processor to run. Each computer has its own assembly language because they work differently. This means assembly code written for one machine won't work on another without changes. Even though it's low-level, assembly language lets programmers access hardware resources like memory, registers, and devices directly. It's often used for creating operating systems, embedded systems, and high-performance applications that need close control over the computer's inner workings. Using symbols in assembly language makes coding easier than machine code, but it requires a good understanding of the computer's structure and instructions to write effectively.
label: llm

text: Chromium(VI) is a harmful substance with oral toxicity levels ranging between 1.5 and 3.3 mg/kg. In our bodies, chromium(VI) gets converted into less harmful chromium(III) before it enters cells. This conversion happens in the bloodstream by various mechanisms. Chromium(III) can then be removed from the body, but chromate ions (a form of chromium(VI)) enter cells using a transport system that also allows sulfate and phosphate ions to enter cells. Chromium(VI)'s toxicity comes from its strong oxidizing properties. Once it enters the bloodstream, it can harm kidneys, liver, and blood cells through oxidation reactions. This can lead to conditions like hemolysis (rupture of red blood cells), renal (kidney) failure, and liver failure. In severe cases, aggressive dialysis might be needed for treatment.

text: In May 1706, during the War of the Spanish Succession, a big fight called the Battle of Ramillies happened near a town in Belgium. Marlborough cleverly outsmarted the French and caught them off guard with a sudden attack on their side. This resulted in many casualties for the French, forcing them to leave the battlefield. The Allies won with around 5,000 casualties, while the French lost about 13,000 men. text: The R5000 is a powerful microprocessor designed by Quantum Effect Design (QED) in 1996, following their earlier models R4600 and R4700. This specific model of microprocessor implements the MIPS IV instruction set architecture (ISA), developed by QED. MIPS Technologies, Inc (MTI) funded this project and later licensed the design to several companies including Integrated Device Technology (IDT), NEC, NKK, and Toshiba. Each company marketed their version of the R5000 under different names: IDT as 79RV5000, NEC as VR5000, NKK as NR5000, and Toshiba as TX5000. After QED was acquired by PMC-Sierra, the R5000 became part of their assets. Today, some versions of the R5000's derivatives are still being produced for use in embedded systems.

text: A Zero-Suppressed Decision Diagram (ZDD) is a helpful tool in computer science for dealing with complex Boolean functions, often found in digital circuit design, model checking, and other computer-aided design areas. It's like an upgraded version of the Binary Decision Diagram (BDD), making it smaller by removing unnecessary nodes that always have a value of 0. This makes it easier to store and work with large Boolean functions. Each node in a ZDD represents either a variable or its opposite, and also has a flag showing if its value is zero. If the flag shows '0', that node gets eliminated, creating a more compact representation of the Boolean function. The ZDD is useful in many fields where managing big Boolean functions efficiently is important.

text: The Brouwer Fixed-Point Theorem is a crucial discovery in the field of topology. This theorem, first introduced by Dutch mathematician L.E.J. Brouwer in 1910, says that any continuous function taking an n-dimensional ball (like a ball in space) and putting it back into itself has at least one point that doesn't move during this process. Imagine a function (f) moving the inside of an n-dimensional sphere (like Earth to Mars and back) onto its surface, then back into the sphere again. There must be a spot within the sphere that stays put under this transformation. This theorem is vital in many areas like math equations, system dynamics, and game theory. It helps find solutions for certain types of problems and gives insights into complex systems' behavior. Over time, other mathematicians have built on Brouwer's work, but the main idea remains a key concept in topology and mathematics.
label: llm

text: In 1798, during President John Adams' term, four laws called the Alien and Sedition Acts were passed by Congress. These laws aimed to ensure national security and loyalty but faced heavy criticism for limiting individual rights and freedoms. The first act, named the Alien Enemies Act, allowed the president to expel any foreign-born person suspected of threatening our country without a fair trial or hearing. The second act, called the Naturalization Act, increased the time needed to become a citizen from five to fourteen years. The third act, known as the Alien Friends Act, gave the president the power to deport anyone deemed dangerous to public safety or order. Lastly, the Sedition Act, the most controversial, made it illegal to publish false statements about the government or its officials, punishable by fines and jail time. Critics argued that these laws were an abuse of federal power and a threat to free speech and press. They believed these acts went too far in limiting our fundamental freedoms.

Over time, Boxing Day transformed into a day off for businesses and organizations. In places like Canada and Australia, it's turned into a big shopping day with lots of sales. text: In some of Sondheim's songs, different performers sing separate tunes at once. This use of counterpoint makes the music stay connected, even when it gets more complicated than a simple round like "Frère Jacques". Sondheim explained that he enjoyed experimenting with this style and creating trios to improve his skills in writing multiple melodies together. He didn't want to follow the quodlibet method, which combines songs that don't usually fit well together. Instead, he cleverly connected three tunes that seemed unlikely to match. A special part of "Soon" helped make this work, with chords having five or six notes. Music expert Steve Swayne noted that these counterpoint sections in the group performances show Sondheim's passion for this musical technique.

text: The Butterfly Effect is a theory from chaos science that explains how tiny actions can lead to big results in complicated systems. It was first thought up by meteorologist Edward Lorenz, who found that even minor changes in weather conditions could cause huge differences in the weather. This idea suggests that something as simple as a butterfly flapping its wings might trigger a hurricane somewhere else in the world. Although this sounds unlikely, it shows how complex systems can be unpredictable and sensitive to small changes at the start. In other words, even the smallest change can set off a chain reaction, making it harder to predict what will happen over time. This idea has been used in many fields like physics, biology, economics, and social sciences to explain how small events can have big impacts on complex systems.

The imperial order to use it for Arian services was withdrawn again. There were attempts to seize Ambrose, force him out of Milan, and even kidnap him, but no formal charges were ever brought against him. It seems the emperor chose not to act due to Ambrose's widespread popularity among the people, fearing their potential reaction.
label: human

text: In February 1989, the passenger version of the Boeing 747-400 started flying with Northwest Airlines on their Minneapolis to Phoenix route. A few months later, in September, KLM began using a combination passenger and cargo version. The freight-only version didn't start until November 1993 with Cargolux. In October 2002, Air France got the cargo-focused 747-400ERF, and Qantas started using the regular 747-400ER around the same time in November. Then, in January 2004, Boeing teamed up with Cathay Pacific to create a program called Boeing Converted Freighter (BCF), which converted passenger 747-400s into cargo planes. The first BCF was delivered in December 2005.
label: human

text: Organizational units (OUs) are groups that organize items within a domain. They help create a hierarchy and make managing the domain easier, often mirroring an organization's structure in terms of management or location. OUs can even contain other OUs, similar to how domains act as containers. Microsoft suggests using OUs for structure and simplifying policy implementation. Group policies, known as group policy objects (GPOs), are best applied at the OU level, although they can also be used on domains or specific locations. Administrative powers are usually delegated at the OU level, but they can also be given to individual items or attributes.
label: human

text: In the 1930s, a British engineer named Charles Collett designed a type of steam train called the 4073 Class for the Great Western Railway (GWR). One of these trains, numbered 7027 Thornbury Castle, was built in Swindon in 1935 and named after a historic castle near Bristol. For many years, it traveled on different routes across the GWR network, even pulling express trains between London Paddington and the West Country. After Britain's railways were taken over by the government in 1948, the train continued to run under British Railways (BR) ownership until it was retired from service in 1962. After some time in storage, a private owner restored it to working condition and preserved it for heritage operations. Now, this classic steam locomotive belongs to the West Country Railway Association and runs on special events and trips throughout the year, giving steam train fans a chance to enjoy its vintage sights and sounds.
label: llm

text: Confucius was a famous Chinese thinker who lived around 500 BCE in the Lu state (now Shandong Province). He is known for his ideas about morality, relationships, justice, and sincerity in both personal life and government. Education, self-improvement, and good character were very important to him. His thoughts greatly affected East Asian beliefs and culture. People often give Confucius credit for creating Confucianism, a unique philosophy system, although some scholars think he mainly passed on existing wisdom. His teachings are written in the Analects, an important book in East Asian philosophy. Confucius' influence reaches beyond China, shaping politics, education, and social customs in many Asian countries.
label: llm

text: Botany is all about understanding plants - their parts, how they grow, change, reproduce, and use energy. It's a big subject that includes learning about plant structure, growth, and behavior, as well as their chemistry, genetics, and the way they live in different environments. Scientists called botanists study many things related to plants, like photosynthesis (how plants make food), how they take in nutrients, and how they respond to changes around them. They also look at the variety of plant species we have, where they're found, and how they interact with other living things. Botany is important for many areas, such as farming, gardening, forestry, medicine, and conservation. To learn more about plants, botanists use tools like microscopes, light spectroscopy, and molecular biology. Their work helps us understand the complex ways plants interact with their surroundings and leads to new technologies and products made from plants. So, botany remains a vital field of study in today's science world.
label: llm

text: In the years 892, 893, and 899, Arnulf was unable to fully conquer Great Moravia. However, he did have some victories, most notably in 895 when Bohemia, now a separate duchy, became a vassal state under his rule after an agreement with Duke Borivoj I. This move protected Bohemia from potential Frankish invasions. Around the same time, it's believed that Great Moravia lost part of its territory, present-day western Hungary, to Arnulf. As a token of gratitude, Wiching was made Arnulf's chancellor in 892. In his quest to conquer Moravia, Arnulf sought help from the Magyars who lived in the Carpathian Basin in 899, and with their aid, he managed to exert some control over Moravia.
label: human

text: Babrak Karmal was an Afghan politician who became the leader of his country after a revolution in 1978. Born in 1929, he studied law and later joined the People's Democratic Party of Afghanistan (PDPA). He played a big role in the revolution that ended the monarchy. After the Soviet Union invaded Afghanistan in 1979, Karmal became the leader of the PDPA and President of Afghanistan, chosen by the new government backed by the Soviets. During his presidency, he worked to make changes for socialism and modernize the economy, but many people opposed him due to his ties with Islamist and nationalist groups. He was also closely connected to the Soviet Union, which helped his government with military and economic support. In 1986, Karmal stepped down as President, reportedly because he disagreed with leaders of the Soviet Union about how to fight against rebel groups.
label: llm

text: Blizzard Entertainment, based in Irvine, California, is a well-known video game company founded in 1991 by Mike Morhaime, Frank Pearce, and Allen Adham. They started small but quickly grew to be one of the world's top gaming studios. Their first game, "RPG: The Legend of Sean," did well, but it was "Warcraft: Orcs & Humans" in 1994 that really put them on the map. After that, hits like "StarCraft" (1998) and "Diablo II" (2000) made Blizzard a major player in gaming. Their most famous series might be "World of Warcraft," an MMORPG released in 2004 that's now one of the best-selling video games ever. Over the years, they've added popular titles like "Overwatch" (2016) and "Hearthstone" (2014), keeping them at the top of the gaming industry.

text: In some countries, governments might unfairly help certain business owners who are close friends with government officials, often due to shared religion or ethnicity. For example, Alawites in Syria hold a large portion of the country's power in both government and business, with President Assad being an Alawite himself. This can be understood by thinking about relationships as a network. When leaders need help achieving their goals, they usually ask for support from powerful people they know. These influential individuals act like important nodes in this network. In developing countries, these key players might be rare, leading to a small group controlling both economic and political power.
label: human
